paragraphs.body: Cafés Are Good for our Mental Well-being I read the journal entry “Cafes for Life: Are Cafes Good for our Mental Wellbeing?” by Clair Fitzsimmons the author answers the question “are cafes good for our mental wellbeing? “She expresses her answer by first talking about her personal life. She gives us a story about her as a mother and the types of anxiety it can bring but uses this as a wormhole on how even just getting to the cafe gives her a sense of accomplishment. Then she talks about factual studies by Dr. Gillian Sandstorm about weak and strong connections. This was done at a cafe and concluded “that people were more satisfied, connected and happier, if they had engaged the barista, even for just a little while.” She then uses this information to convey that cafes help us make those little connections and are great communication spaces. How it gives us the opportunity “-the main way we get to be in the world while deciding how and if we interact with other people.” She then ends her journal giving us examples of cafes that help people with different things such as Sip of Hope, which profits go to suicide prevention 100% and Happy Cafes that associate with Action for Happiness. Overall, the author is trying to give people an outlet that may help their mental health overtime like it had helped her. However, cafes aren’t the first outlet I think when it comes to my mental health. Cafés are not the only places for connections. It can be in all different types of places like the bus, school, and library. Though Clair’s writing goes into more than the place but the connections themselves. She emphasizes that by saying “The work of connection, of putting people in front of us, with our nods and their smiles, our how are you's and what about the weathers. They help us.” This quote is important to me because it indicates how small interactions are big. They are what’s important and what keeps us going through the hard times. Even the ones we don’t dare to talk about. I relate to this on a heavy level.

paragraphs.body: My life now and back in middle school of 2019 is very different. Right now, I have friends I can talk to, make social connections, and talk to a cashier. Back then I was very timid and the thought of talking to someone I didn’t know would bring me to tears. I had trouble making meaningful connections that stuck with me. Now I can see that a lot of people liked me, and I wasn’t as alone as I thought but, back then, it wasn’t so clear. I started branching out around eighth grade but before I could I got sick, covid happened, and I switched schools. I completely isolated myself from my room. Sure, I talked to people, but it was through a screen and people I barely knew but their online persona. I didn’t truly get the meaning of a connection till ninth grade high school. I felt like a robot that crashed at the sign of a conversation. Even ordering food was difficult. I wouldn’t say I was the most extroverted kid before covid, but I wasn’t so timid.

annotations.text: say I was the most extroverted kid before covid, but I wasn’t so timid.
annotations.comment: This is an interesting line that shows the "hidden" impacts of Covid. Nice job.

paragraphs.body: Covid took a lot away from society, one of the big ones being communication. People were glued to their phones and technology in general for a few years. It sets a lot of people back, especially kids. When it comes to reading, writing, and talking to actual people. It made it hard for people to make friends too. People had their online personas but in person they were much different. For me this was overwhelming and so I decided not to speak to people or not to let them know the real me. It took me years to get out of this habit and truthfully, I’m not sure I fully stopped. I had to catch public transit once a week to go to school in person. I was talking to people, yet I still felt very alone. I was urning for a connection with a true one, someone to really care for who cares back. But to get there I had to practice and get comfortable with that feeling. Every day in the morning I would go to a café near my school and practice. It started off with me just ordering what I wanted to drink or eat. To compliments and add a little bit of conversation. To sometimes have a full-blown conversation with ease. Though I still get flustered and have to amp myself up to do it. Another way I achieved this was to spend hours in the library and at some point, someone is bound to talk to you. Even about little things and it’s a quiet and relaxing place to feel comfortable. Eventually I got the courage to make that connection I wanted to make and maintain it. I took a long time to do so almost four years, but it was worth it. It had been something I’ve almost forgotten.

paragraphs.body: Clair Fitzsimmons journal entry reminded me of this. Especially the part about the study done by Dr Gillian Sandstorm who tested whether minimal social interactions were good for our wellbeing. The conclusion was according to Clair she stated that “Her study concluded that people were more satisfied, connected and happier, if they had engaged the barista, even for just a little while.”. I found this information helpful because I could relate it to my own life. Talking to baristas does boost my mood. It does help that I have a friend as one. Small talk is a connection and boosts our day positively even if it’s a little. Speaking to people makes us more humane and keeps us going. Without connections we wouldn’t succeed as a society.

annotations.text: Small talk is a connection and boosts our day positively even if it’s a little. Speaking to people makes us more humane and keeps us going. Without connections we wouldn’t succeed as a society.
annotations.comment: This is a great point, and you've made me think! I usually don't like small talk, but I'm seeing a new value to it because of your essay :)

paragraphs.body: Not everywhere has places like this. Buying coffee is a privilege but going to festivals, libraries, parks, and museums you connect with people while you’re ‘there. Whether it is with words or not. Sometimes a bit of eye contact shows understanding. Though I tend to avoid that as much as possible. Even if there is silence that is enough. Words don’t need to be said, just a sense of understanding and togetherness. A sense where you belong, and you can keep on pushing even through hardship. That is hard, and you may feel one but, in the end, you’ll look back at the connections you made and be astonished by what you have become and gone through. Realizing that even one connection is important.
